feedback.text: As a consumer and citizen of the European Union and thanks to the Stop Killing Games initiative, it would be wonderful if the Digital Fairness Act collected consumer protection from the consumption of art, culture and entertainment, which is the medium of video games. The lobby representing some of the industry has already expressed its intention to fight for the continued advarition of the world, saying that it is unacceptable for companies to provide alternatives to turn off their servers and forget. Nothing further from reality, many games are launched with tools that are more than sufficient to consider them as life plans after the end of the developer’s support. I will give two examples: Dune awakening, developed by Funcom, Norway, will no longer have to worry about supporting as it allows players to create their own private servers. This video game came out this year and maintains the good practice of providing its players with this very widespread possibility in their generation. Trackla, developed by Nadeo, France. It is an ip that players in each of their games have been able to enjoy the competition of recording the best times to date. They have spent more than 20 years, thus generating one of the most resilient communities with the best social fabric I have ever known. In addition to community-generated tools, Nadeo has always made games available locally, where its users are free to create their own servers and simply maintain a database with the best times for each runway. The latter is unnecessary but demonstrates the love that developers have for their own game. Both games have models of GaSS- game as a service. Like them, there are as many others. It shows not only that it is possible to protect culture in the environment, as well as the fact that they are centres of unity between Europeans from different countries where they share a passion and create a social and community fabric that is far removed from the issues of hatred so recently. Thank you, an abrasion.
